concept.headline: Kotlin's core types — Int, Double, Boolean, Char, String — are all objects. String templates let you embed values directly inside strings.
concept.explanation: Unlike Java, Kotlin has no primitive types at the language level — everything is an object, though the compiler optimizes them to JVM primitives under the hood. The main numeric types are Int (32-bit), Long (64-bit), Double (64-bit float), and Float (32-bit float). String templates ($name or ${expr}) avoid messy concatenation.
concept.syntax:
val age: Int     = 25
val height: Double = 1.75
val active: Boolean = true
val initial: Char   = 'A'
val name: String    = "Alice"

// String templates
println("$name is $age")          // simple var
println("${name.length} letters") // expression inside ${}
keyPoints: Boolean: true or false — no integers used as booleans
Char uses single quotes: 'A'. String uses double quotes: "hello"
String templates: $varName or ${expression} — no concatenation needed

demo.intro: Watch how different types are stored and how a string template assembles them into one output line.
demo.code:
fun main() {
    val name: String  = "Alice"
    val age: Int      = 25
    val height: Double = 1.75
    val active: Boolean = true
    println("$name | age=$age | height=$height | active=$active")
}

explanation.why: Stores the player's name as a String — sequence of characters in double quotes
explanation.misconception: String in Kotlin is not a char array — it is an immutable object. You cannot change individual characters of a String.

explanation.why: Stores a whole number. Int covers -2,147,483,648 to 2,147,483,647
explanation.intent: Introduce Int — the most common integer type
explanation.misconception: Do not use Long unless the value exceeds Int's range. Using Long everywhere wastes memory and is not idiomatic Kotlin.

explanation.why: Stores a decimal number. Kotlin defaults floating-point literals to Double
explanation.intent: Introduce Double — the default fractional type
explanation.misconception: A plain decimal literal like 1.75 is a Double by default. To make it a Float you'd write 1.75f. Mixing Float and Double in an expression requires explicit conversion.

explanation.why: Stores a yes/no flag — Boolean holds only true or false
explanation.intent: Introduce Boolean — the type for logical values
explanation.misconception: Unlike C/C++, you cannot use 1/0 as Booleans in Kotlin. Only true and false are valid — the compiler rejects anything else.

explanation.what: println with a string template inserts all four variables
explanation.why: String templates read $name, $age, $height, $active and embed their string representations
explanation.intent: Show how string templates unify all types into one readable output
explanation.misconception: You do not need to call .toString() on each variable — string template handles it automatically by calling each value's toString() implementation

prediction.question:
What is the output?

val x = 5
val y = 2.0
println(x + y)
options: Compile error — cannot add Int and Double
prediction.explanation: Kotlin does NOT automatically widen Int to Double in arithmetic. You must convert explicitly: x.toDouble() + y. This is intentional — implicit numeric conversion is a common source of bugs in other languages.

explanation.afterDemo: Every variable in Kotlin has a type — even when you don't write it. The compiler infers it from the value. Kotlin's strict type system catches type mismatches at compile time, not at runtime, which eliminates a whole class of bugs. String templates are idiomatic and much cleaner than concatenation.
keyTakeaways: Core types: Int, Long, Double, Float, Boolean, Char, String — all objects under the hood.
No implicit numeric conversion — use .toInt(), .toDouble() etc. when mixing types.
String templates ($var or ${expr}) are the idiomatic way to build strings in Kotlin.

practice.prompt: Declare val product = "Laptop", val price: Double = 999.99, val inStock: Boolean = true. Print: "Laptop costs $999.99, in stock: true".
practice.hint: Use $$ to print a literal dollar sign inside a string template.

feedback.successMessage: Correct! Note the $$ trick to print a literal $ character inside a string template.
retryHints: Declare: val product = "Laptop", val price: Double = 999.99, val inStock: Boolean = true
To print a literal dollar sign inside a string template write $$
Expected: Laptop costs $999.99, in stock: true

summary.title: Data Types — Complete
takeaways: Int, Double, Boolean, Char, String are the core everyday types.
Kotlin has no implicit numeric conversion — be explicit when mixing types.
String templates ($var or ${expr}) are the idiomatic way to embed values in strings.
